segments.title: Reading the Imperial Fleet as a naval system
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke sets out to assess the Warhammer 40,000 Imperial Fleet as a working navy, including its formations, roles, and capabilities.
segments.body: The introduction frames the Imperial Fleet as more than a collection of dramatic ship designs. Dr. Clarke proposes to look at how its formations and capabilities would function together, using Battlefleet Gothic imagery to inspect the ships. That approach brings operational purpose and fleet-level viability into a discussion usually dominated by visual design.

evidence.note: He explains that he is using Battlefleet Gothic to build an image of the fleet, its formations, operations, and capabilities.

segments.title: Why no ship should ignore its rear arc
segments.summary: The Imperial Fleet's inability to fire astern becomes a critique of fire arcs, layout choices, and the logistical cost of mixed gun systems.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the lack of rearward fire as a fundamental design weakness rather than a cosmetic quirk. A fleet cannot assume that an enemy will remain in front of it, and broad hull structures appear to leave room for stern-facing weapons. He also uses the mixed-gun turret to underline a practical concern: unusual weapon combinations complicate logistics and maintenance even when they fit the setting's fiction.

evidence.note: He identifies the lack of rearward weapons, proposes stern locations for them, and criticizes the mixed-gun turret from a logistics and maintenance perspective.

segments.title: Modern warships still need stern defence
segments.summary: A brief comparison with modern close-in weapons systems explains why rearward coverage and redundancy matter in warship design.
segments.body: The closing comparison connects the fictional criticism to a real design problem. Modern warships need close-in coverage toward the stern because an attack may come from that direction, especially around hangar arrangements. Dr. Clarke also points to the value of alternative arcs: a pair of weapons placed to either side can preserve coverage by turning the ship if one mount fails.

evidence.note: He compares stern coverage, close-in weapons, side-mounted arrangements, and the effect of a failed mount.
